articles.title: Fuel Shortages Stall Venezuela Rescue Efforts as Cranes Sit Idle

articles.title: How AI Creates Jobs
articles.summary: Lisa Simon, Chief Economist at Revelio Labs joined Bloomberg Open Interest to explain why a weaker-than-expected jobs report doesn't tell the whole story. Using hiring data from LinkedIn, Glassdoor, H-1B visa filings, she argues AI is helping fuel hiring, She explains why companies investing most heavily in AI are expanding both entry-level and experienced roles. (Source: Bloomberg)

articles.title: Over 20 million expected in Tehran for Ali Khamenei's funeral
articles.summary: Over 20 million expected in Tehran for Ali Khamenei's funeral More than 20 million people are reportedly expected to travel to Iran’s capital for the funeral of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, who was assassinated by the US and Israel on 28 February, according to Iranian state TV. This would more than double Tehran’s population of roughly 10 million. Traffic police stand guard under a giant billboard bearing a picture of Iran's late Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in Baghdad on 2 July 2026 (Ahmad al-Rubaye/AFP)

articles.summary: OIC condemns Israeli bill restricting Muslim call to prayer The general secretariat of the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation (OIC) on Thursday condemned the Israeli Knesset’s preliminary approval of a bill to restrict the Muslim call to prayer. Under the proposal, mosques would be prohibited from installing or operating loudspeaker systems without a permit granted by Israeli authorities. The OIC described the proposed legislation as a discriminatory and racist measure that constitutes a flagrant violation of freedom of religion and worship guaranteed under international law. The bill passed by 50 votes to 36 and must now be referred to a Knesset committee before returning for its first reading. It would then need to pass second and third readings before becoming law. articles.title: How Iran won the World Cup (or didn't lose it anyway)
articles.summary: How Iran won the World Cup (or didn't lose it anyway) Iran was knocked out of the World Cup on Saturday, yet the government’s now calling it a victory. Whether or not it’s the moral victory they say it is, a tidal wave of international support points to a plausible PR victory. Iran’s football fate was sealed by two last-minute plot twists, earning them the BBC title of "Unluckiest team in World Cup history".  But global commentators hit back hard: not “luck”, they said, but “sabotage” and “discrimination”. Was Iran the unluckiest team in World Cup history - or the worst treated? And while
